The modeling guide helps you to build architecture by providing summary information about the UAF structure and architecture creation process.</span></li><li data-uuid="dc541f1d-3b62-4a1f-8d49-46ad42bcf280"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><strong>EA Project Template with Process Guide</strong> - using this template, you will get a new UAF project enhanced with the Enterprise Architecture Guide for UAF (a non-normative component of the UAF specification). This modeling guide aids in building architecture by providing nine steps of workflow.</span></li><li data-uuid="83103e08-8cf2-4c21-b147-5784077a4959"><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><strong>Blank UAF Project</strong> - creates a blank UAF project.</span></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="warning" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="36302a13-0993-4e01-9260-10ef8aff2505"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Please make sure, that the UAF 1.3 Plugin is <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="IL" ri:content-title="Installing plugins" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[installed]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br /></p><hr /><p>*Starting with the 2024x version, the <strong style="text-align: left;">UAF Enterprise Architecture Project</strong><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);"><span> </span>was renamed to<span> </span></span><strong style="text-align: left;">EA with BPMN Project.</strong></p><p><strong style="text-align: left;">      </strong></p><p><ac:image ac:width="1011"><ri:attachment ri:filename="Grid V1.3.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">UAF 1.3 Grid. The grid is a way of showing how the various view specifications (cells) correspond to viewpoints (prev. known as domains) (horizontal rows) and the aspects (prev. known as model kinds) (the columns) that describe the view specification. </h6><p>The descriptions of all the viewpoints are provided in the following table:</p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 93.8014%;"><colgroup class=""> <col class="" style="width: 7.89936%;" /> <col class="" style="width: 4.73961%;" /> <col class="" style="width: 87.361%;" /> </colgroup><tbody class=""><tr class=""><th><p align="center"><strong>Viewpoint</strong></p></th><th><p align="center"><strong>Acronym</strong></p></th><th><p align="center"><strong>Description</strong></p></th></tr><tr class=""><th><p>Architecture Management</p></th><td style="text-align: center;"><p>Am</p></td><td><p>Identifies the metadata required to develop a suitable architecture that is fit for its purpose.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th><p>Strategic</p></th><td style="text-align: center;"><p>St</p></td><td><p>Capability management process. Describes the capability taxonomy, composition, dependencies, and evolution.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th><p>Operational</p></th><td style="text-align: center;"><p>Op</p></td><td><p>Illustrates the Logical Architecture of the enterprise. Describes the requirements, operational behavior, structure, and exchanges required to support (exhibit) capabilities. Defines all operational elements in an implementation/solution-independent manner.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th><p>Services</p></th><td style="text-align: center;"><p>Sv</p></td><td><p>The Service-Orientated View (SOV) is a description of services needed to directly support the operational domain as described in the Operational View. A service within</p><p>MODAF is understood in its broadest sense, as a unit of work through which a provider provides a useful result to a consumer.</p><p>DoDAF: The Service Views within the Services Viewpoint describe the design for service-based solutions to support operational development processes (JCIDS) and Defense Acquisition System or capability development within the Joint Capability Areas.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th><p>Personnel</p></th><td style="text-align: center;"><p>Ps</p></td><td><p>Defines and explores organizational resource types. Shows the taxonomy of types of organizational resources as well as connections, interaction, and growth over time.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th><p>Resources</p></th><td style="text-align: center;"><p>Rs</p></td><td><p>Captures a solution architecture consisting of resources, e.g., organizational, software, artifacts, capability configurations, and natural resources that implement the operational requirements. Further design of a resource is typically detailed in SysML v1 or UML.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th><p>Security</p></th><td style="text-align: center;"><p>Sc</p></td><td><p>Security assets and security enclaves. Defines the hierarchy of security assets and asset owners, security constraints (policy, laws, and guidance), and details where they are located (security enclaves).</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th><p>Projects</p></th><td style="text-align: center;"><p>Pj</p></td><td><p>Describes projects and project milestones, how those projects deliver capabilities, the organizations contributing to the projects, and dependencies between projects.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th><p>Standards</p></th><td style="text-align: center;"><p>Sd</p></td><td><p>MODAF: Technical Standards Views are extended from the core DoDAF views to include non-technical standards such as operational doctrine, industry process standards, etc.</p><p>DoDAF: The Standards Views within the Standards Viewpoint are the set of rules governing the arrangement, interaction, and interdependence of solution parts or elements.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th><p>Actual Resources</p></th><td style="text-align: center;"><p>Ar</p></td><td><p>The analysis, e.g., evaluation of different alternatives, what-if, trade-offs, V&amp;V on the actual resource configurations. Illustrates the expected or achieved actual resource configurations.</p></td></tr></tbody></table><p><br /></p><p>The descriptions of all the aspects are provided in the following table:</p><table class="relative-table" style="width: 94.0208%;"><colgroup class=""><col class="" style="width: 7.82253%;" /><col class="" style="width: 5.02043%;" /><col class="" style="width: 87.157%;" /></colgroup><tbody class=""><tr class=""><th style="text-align: center;">Aspect</th><th style="text-align: center;">Acronym</th><th style="text-align: center;">Description</th></tr><tr class=""><th colspan="1">Motivation</th><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">Mv</td><td colspan="1">Captures motivational elements e.g., challenges, opportunities, and concerns, that pertain to enterprise transformation efforts, and different types of requirements, e.g., operational, services, personnel, resources, or security controls.</td></tr><tr class=""><th>Taxonomy</th><td style="text-align: center;">Tx</td><td><p>Presents all the elements as a standalone structure. Presents all the elements as a specialization hierarchy, provides a text definition for each one and references the<strong> </strong>source of the element.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th>Structure</th><td style="text-align: center;">Sr</td><td><p>Describes the breakdown of structural elements e.g. logical performers, systems, projects, etc. into their smaller parts.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th>Connectivity</th><td style="text-align: center;">Cn</td><td><p>Describes the connections, relationships, and interactions between the different elements.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th colspan="1">Processes</th><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">Pr</td><td colspan="1"><p>Captures activity-based behavior and flows. It describes activities, their Inputs/Outputs, activity actions, and flows between them.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th colspan="1">States</th><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">St</td><td colspan="1"><p>Captures state-based behavior of an element. It is a graphical representation of states of a structural element and how it responds to various events and actions.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th colspan="1">Sequences</th><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">Sq</td><td colspan="1"><p>Expresses a time-ordered examination of the exchanges as a result of a particular scenario. Provides a time-ordered examination of the exchanges between participating elements as a result of a particular scenario.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th colspan="1">Information</th><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">If</td><td colspan="1"><p>Address the information perspective on operational, service, and resource architectures. Allows analysis of an architecture’s information and data definition aspect, without consideration of implementation-specific issues.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th colspan="1">Parameters</th><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">Pm</td><td colspan="1">Shows the measurable properties of something in the physical world and elements and relationships that are involved in defining the environments applicable to capability, operational concept or set of systems.</td></tr><tr class=""><th colspan="1">Constraints</th><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">Ct</td><td colspan="1"><p>Details the measurements that set performance requirements constraining capabilities. Also defines the rules governing behavior and structure.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th colspan="1">Roadmap</th><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">Rm</td><td colspan="1"><p>Addresses how elements in the architecture change over time.</p></td></tr><tr class=""><th colspan="1">Traceability</th><td style="text-align: center;" colspan="1">Tr</td><td colspan="1"><p>Describes the mapping between elements in the architecture. This can be between different viewpoints within domains as well as between domains. It can also be between structure and behaviors.</p></td></tr></tbody></table></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>UAF 1.3 viewpoints</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="e448b33b-8517-4791-9a4c-0a2fe6f8dfdf" /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249565339 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 06500: UAF Behavior to structure synchronization

- page_id: `249565339`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249565339/UAF+Behavior+to+structure+synchronization
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-18T17:07:44.847+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > Procedures
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

The behavior to structure synchronization checks the consistency of the <viewpoint name>***** Internal Connectivity diagram with Activities, automatically detects inconsistencies, and enables users to handle each inconsistency individually by providing solver actions to ensure the coherence of the <viewpoint name>***** Internal Connectivity diagrams with Activities.

600400

The behavior to structure synchronization validates the Operational / Resource / Service Role displayed in the <viewpoint name>***** Internal Connectivity Diagram to check for compatible

- Operational / Resource / Service Ports
- Operational / Resource / Service Connectors
- Operational / Resource / Service Interfaces
- Operational / Resource / Service Exchanges

To verify whether or not the structural elements (e.g., ports, interfaces) and behavioral elements (e.g., flows) are compatible, validation rules are used. If any inconsistencies are found, they are highlighted in yellow in the Internal Connectivity Diagram and displayed in the**Validation Results** panel. Then, they can be individually handled using solvers.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### Synchronizing behavior to the structure.

##### Checking behavior to structure synchronization

To initiate the behavior to structure synchronization check, ensure the Internal Connectivity Diagrams have

displayed,

).

To check the structure model consistency with the behavior model

1. In the toolbar of the <viewpoint name> * Internal Connectivity Diagram, click [ATTACHMENT filename='tools_button.png'] Tools.
2. In the open drop-down menu, select Behavior to Structure Synchronization. [IMAGE alt='' src='']

The behavior to structure synchronization check is initiated.

*** <Viewpoint name>** - Operational, Personnel, Resources, Services, Security (Operational and Resource).

**Continue reading**

**Related diagrams**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with Operational Internal Connectivity diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with Services Internal Connectivity diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with Personnel Internal Connectivity diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with Resources Internal Connectivity diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with Security Internal Connectivity (Operational) diagram' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Working with Security Internal Connectivity (Resource) diagram' space='']

If any inconsistencies are found, they are highlighted in yellow in the Internal Connectivity Diagram and displayed in the<strong> Validation Results</strong> panel. Then, they can be individually handled using solvers.</p><p><ac:image ac:align="center" ac:width="1419"><ri:attachment ri:filename="UAF BtoSSync Reprezentation.png" /></ac:image></p><h6 style="text-align: center;">Synchronizing behavior to the structure.</h6><h3>Checking behavior to structure synchronization </h3><ac:structured-macro ac:name="note" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2c9205c4-d737-42c3-afbf-a049507df07d"><ac:rich-text-body><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">To initiate the behavior to structure synchronization check, ensure the Internal Connectivity Diagrams have </span>Asset Roles<em style="text-align: left;"> </em><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">displayed,</span><span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);"> and the behavior model is defined (in the Process Flow diagrams, the communication between<span> </span></span>Asset Roles is modeled<span style="color: rgb(23,43,77);">).</span></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><br />To check the structure model consistency with the behavior model</p><hr /><ol><li>In the toolbar of the &lt;viewpoint name&gt;<strong>*</strong> Internal Connectivity Diagram, click <ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="tools_button.png" /></ac:image>Tools.</li><li>In the open drop-down menu, select <strong>Behavior to Structure Synchronization.</strong><br /><strong><ac:image><ri:attachment ri:filename="behavior_to_structure_sync_button.png" /></ac:image><br /></strong></li></ol><p>The behavior to structure synchronization check is initiated. </p><p>  </p><hr /><p><strong>* &lt;Viewpoint name&gt;</strong> - Operational, Personnel, Resources, Services, Security (Operational and Resource).</p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Continue reading</strong></p><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="b14b8c53-e90a-4b72-af8d-2e24f89e8d44" /></p><p><strong>Related diagrams</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with Operational Internal Connectivity diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Operational Internal Connectivity diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with Services Internal Connectivity diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Services Internal Connectivity diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with Personnel Internal Connectivity diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Personnel Internal Connectivity diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with Resources Internal Connectivity diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Resources Internal Connectivity diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with Security Internal Connectivity (Operational) diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Security Internal Connectivity (Operational) diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Working with Security Internal Connectivity (Resource) diagram" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Security Internal Connectivity (Resource) diagram]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249565359 space=MT version=4 -->
## PAGE 06501: UAF element numbering

- page_id: `249565359`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249565359/UAF+element+numbering
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-08-25T11:03:24.834+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > Procedures
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Our modeling tools provide a generic numbering mechanism that can be applied to any elements created in the modeling tool. The generic numbering mechanism allows you to:

- Automatically assign a unique number to the elements in the model when they are created, moved, or your model is refactored.
- Easily identify and find an element in the model when communicating with colleagues or stakeholders.
- Show element's place in a hierarchy.

For more information about the generic numbering mechanism and how to use it, see the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generic numbering mechanism' space=''] documentation.

The default numbering schemas can have two different numbering styles:

- conse **cutive** - this style is to number elements in one numbering level. Specifyonly one number part.
- multi-level - this style is to number elements in several numbering levels. Specify at least one number part for each numbering level. Top level elements will be numbered using the first number part. Elements in subsequent levels (directly or indirectly contained in the top level element) will be numbered using successive number parts.

To learn more about the numbering styles, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Numbering Scheme property values' space='UPDG'] documentation.

UAF numbering uses **multi-level** numbering style schema property.

#### UAF hierarchical numbering

Numbering of UAF motivational elements and information elements is hierarchical.

A hierarchical structure can only contain elements of the same type. Different element types cannot be mixed within the same hierarchy.

Motivational Elements:

- Driver (prefix is D)
- Opportunity (prefix is O)
- Challenge (prefix is C)
- Effect (prefix is E)

Information Elements:

- Operational Information (prefix is OI)
- Resource Information (prefix is RI)
- Strategic Information (prefix is SI)

#### UAF hierarchical numbering with dynamic prefixes

Numbering of UAF elements based on SysML v1 requirements is hierarchical or dynamic (when prefix differs, numbering adapts. numbers will change, but prefixes will be kept according to elements).

A hierarchical structure can contain elements of different type. For example, Enterprise Goal and Enterprise Objective can both be in the same hierarchical structure. The same is with the Security elements.

Strategic Elements:

- Enterprise Goal (prefix is EG)
- Enterprise Objective (prefix is EO)

Security Elements:

- Security Control (prefix is SC)
- Enhanced Security Control (prefix is ESC)
- Security Control Family (prefix is SCF)

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Generic numbering mechanism' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>Our modeling tools provide a generic numbering mechanism that can be applied to any elements created in the modeling tool. The generic numbering mechanism allows you to:</p><ul><li>Automatically assign a unique number to the elements in the model when they are created, moved, or your model is refactored.</li><li>Easily identify and find an element in the model when communicating with colleagues or stakeholders.</li><li>Show element's place in a hierarchy.</li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="7a60c08f-6ebe-4283-a166-55e4cf8ae3de"><ac:rich-text-body><p>For more information about the generic numbering mechanism and how to use it, see the <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Generic numbering mechanism" /></ac:link> documentation.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>The default <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b837aa41-4b6a-43fa-8052-ea7293365d18">numbering</ac:inline-comment-marker> schemas can have two different numbering styles:</p><ul><li><strong>conse</strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="bef9ad5d-ed4a-4f76-8438-cd73baa3324d"><strong>cutive</strong> - this style is to <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">number elements in one numbering level. Specify<span> </span></span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">only one number part.</span></ac:inline-comment-marker></li><li><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="bef9ad5d-ed4a-4f76-8438-cd73baa3324d">multi-le</ac:inline-comment-marker>vel</strong> - this style is <span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">to number elements in several numbering levels. Specify at least one number part for each numbering level. </span><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Top level elements will be numbered using the first number part. Elements in subsequent levels (directly or indirectly contained in the top level element) will be numbered using successive number parts.</span></li></ul><ac:structured-macro ac:name="info" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="148ffb90-68bf-4b29-be24-74712b4da5db"><ac:rich-text-body><p>To learn more about the numbering styles, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:space-key="UPDG" ri:content-title="Numbering Scheme property values" /></ac:link> documentation.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">UAF numbering uses <strong>multi-level</strong> numbering style schema property.</span></p><h2>UAF hierarchical numbering</h2><p>Numbering of UAF motivational elements and information <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="8482ff7a-d0f3-4175-a938-7cd8dbbde8ef">elements is hierarchical. </ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="8482ff7a-d0f3-4175-a938-7cd8dbbde8ef">A hierarchical structure can only contain elements of the same type. Different element types cannot be mixed within the same hierarchy.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Motivational Elements:</span></p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li>Driver (prefix is D)</li><li>Opportunity (prefix is O)</li><li>Challenge (prefix is C)</li><li>Effect (prefix is E)</li></ul><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Information Elements: </span></p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li>Operational Information (prefix is OI)</li><li>Resource Information (prefix is RI)</li><li>Strategic Information (prefix is SI)</li></ul><h2>UAF hierarchical numbering with dynamic prefixes</h2><p>Numbering of UAF <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="78a84ff1-a965-4790-b198-6a7c92402db0">elements based on SysML v1 requirement</ac:inline-comment-marker>s is hierarchical or dynamic (when prefix differs, numbering adapts. numbers will change, but prefixes will be kept according to elements).</p><p>A hierarchical structure can contain elements of different type. For example, Enterprise Goal and Enterprise Objective can both be in the same hierarchical structure. The same is with the Security elements.</p><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Strategic Elements:</span></p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li>Enterprise Goal (prefix is EG)</li><li>Enterprise Objective (prefix is EO)</li></ul><p><span style="color:var(--ds-text,#172b4d);">Security Elements:</span></p><ul style="text-align: left;"><li>Security Control (prefix is SC)</li><li>Enhanced Security Control (prefix is ESC)</li><li>Security Control Family (prefix is SCF)</li></ul><p><ac:image ac:align="center"><ri:attachment ri:filename="hierarchical_element_numbering.png" /></ac:image></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Generic numbering mechanism" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

## PAGE 06502: UAF projects migration manual

- page_id: `249565449`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249565449/UAF+projects+migration+manual

- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling > Migration User Guides

This manual aims to help you understand the differences between UAF specifications and guide you along the data mapping process.

The following chapters describe how migration works.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249565429 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06503: UAF specific elements

- page_id: `249565429`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249565429/UAF+specific+elements
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T16:29:42.186+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > UAF 1.3 modeling
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

| Metaclass | Name | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Association | Operational Association | A relationship summarizing Operational Exchanges between connected Operational Performers. |
| Resource Association | A relationship summarizing Resource Exchanges between connected Resources. |  |
| Call Behavior Action | Operational Action | A Call Behavior Action for which the default type will not be created. However if you need to create or set a type for this element, you can select or create only an Operational Activity. |
| Resource Action | A Call Behavior Action for which the default type will not be created. However if you need to create or set a type for this element, you can select or create only a Function. |  |
| Service Action | A Call Behavior Action for which the default type will not be created. However if you need to create or set a type for this element, you can select or create only a Service Function. |  |
| Class | Time Period | An element which contains Date elements. |
| Dependency | Arbitrary Relationship Dashed Left | A relationship which you can used in a Free Form diagram to show dependencies. |
| Arbitrary Relationship Dashed Right |  |  |
| Arbitrary Relationship Directed Left |  |  |
| Arbitrary Relationship Directed Right |  |  |
| Arbitrary Relationship Solid |  |  |
| Property | Agreement Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Agreement. |
| Capability Configuration Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Capability Configuration. |  |
| Condition Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Condition. |  |
| Environment Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Environment. |  |
| Location Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Location. |  |
| Natural Resource Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Natural Resource. |  |
| Operational Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Operational Performer. |  |
| Organization Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Organization. |  |
| Person Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Person. |  |
| Post Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Post. |  |
| Resource Artifact Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Resource Artifact. |  |
| Software Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Software. |  |
| Technology Concept Role | A Concept Role which type is Technology. |  |

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243973870 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06505: UML 2.5.1 support

- page_id: `243973870`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243973870/UML+2.5.1+support
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T19:19:29.188+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

771348400

771348405

771348403

Our modeling tools always support the latest versions of UML standards. Version 19.0 supports changes in the UML specification from version 2.5 to 2.5.1.

UML 2.5.1 provides several minor enhancements:

- Vertexes are redefinable in State Machines. A vertex is either a State, or Pseudo State, or Connection Point Reference. In earlier standard versions, only the State was redefinable.
- The semantic of Context and Constrained Element properties of the Constraint are clarified in the standard specification.
- The Behavior of an Opaque Expression is now allowed to have input parameters.

The latter two items do not affect the modeling tool functionality.

771348389

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Profiling changes' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html

Version 19.0 supports changes in the UML specification from version 2.5 to 2.5.1.</p><p>UML 2.5.1 provides several minor enhancements:</p><ul><li>Vertexes are redefinable in State Machines. A vertex is either a State, or Pseudo State, or Connection Point Reference. In earlier standard versions, only the State was redefinable. </li><li>The semantic of Context and Constrained Element properties of the Constraint are clarified in the standard specification.</li><li>The Behavior of an Opaque Expression is now allowed to have input parameters.</li></ul><p>The latter two items do not affect the modeling tool functionality. </p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0ec2eed8-72b4-461b-9616-b9fb1b57c8f2"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">771348389</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Profiling changes" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243966383 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06506: UML diagrams

- page_id: `243966383`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243966383/UML+diagrams
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:49:57.986+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Diagrams
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

In software development, the diagram is the equivalent of a blueprint. To meet varying user requirements, we often need several different "blueprints" of the same system. Every system is described by different aspects, such as:

- Structural
- Behavioral

Structure and Behavior diagrams are comprised of different types of the UML diagrams.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Different types of the UML diagrams.

The modeling tool supports the following diagrams defined in UML 2:

|  |  |  |  |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |
|  |  |  |  |

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=249567362 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06508: UML to SQL transformation

- page_id: `249567362`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/249567362/UML+to+SQL+transformation
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-08-18T18:28:32.044+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > Data-related modeling > Transformations
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

237093793

237093795

237093792

true

237093794

There are two very similar UML to DDL transformations:

1. UML to SQL (Generic)
2. UML to SQL (Oracle)

These transformations convert the selected part of a UML model with class diagrams into Generic or Oracle SQL models with Oracle SQL diagrams respectively.

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-layer" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="a67ba747-9426-4d35-849b-a5df735e4f8d"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">237093793</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-column" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="36ed12f5-1e41-490d-9110-db62e1906892"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">237093795</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="257d72ef-365f-4a72-ae27-bc11f4a2ead5"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">237093792</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="children" ac:schema-version="2" ac:macro-id="a6acd33d-c210-492f-b77e-06ab0eed36e4"><ac:parameter ac:name="all">true</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="content-block" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2022a36f-225f-4964-9076-7b5a0ff2dd49"><ac:parameter ac:name="id">237093794</ac:parameter><ac:rich-text-body><p>There are two very similar UML to DDL transformations:</p><ol><li>UML to SQL (Generic)</li><li>UML to SQL (Oracle)</li></ol><p>These transformations convert the selected part of a UML model with class diagrams into Generic or Oracle SQL models with Oracle SQL diagrams respectively.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro>
````

- version_date: `2025-08-18T18:32:24.714+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > Data-related modeling > Transformations
- labels: ['msosa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

34012851

34012854

34012852

The UML to XML Schema transformation helps create an equivalent XML schema model from a given UML model. Basically, this transformation creates a copy of a source UML model, then applies the necessary stereotypes according to the XML schema modeling rules.

##### **Type mapping**

This type map stores mapping between primitive UML data types and primitive XML Schema data types.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### UML to XML schema type map.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']UML to XML schema type map (Continued).

##### **Transformation results**

After the UML models have been transformed to XML schema models, you will see the following results.

- For each class in the transformation destination set, the «XSDcomplexType» stereotype is applied, unless this class is derived from simple XML type (one of the basic types, or type, stereotyped with XSDsimpleType). In that case, an «XSDsimpleType» stereotype is applied. There are conditions when the «XSDcomplexType» and «XSDsimpleType» stereotypes are used, listed in the table below:

| Condition | Result |
| --- | --- |
| The class is derived from another class, stereotyped as «XSDcomplexType». | The «XSDcomplexContent» stereotype is applied on this class with «XSDextension» on the corresponding generalization relationship. |
| The class is derived from another class, stereotyped as «XSDsimpleType». | The «XSDrestriction» stereotype is applied on the corresponding generalization relationship. |
| The class is not derived from anything, and has attributes with the XSDelement tag. | The «XSDcomplexContent» stereotype is applied on this class. |
| The class is not derived from anything, and has no attributes with the XSDelement tag. | No «XXXXContent» stereotype is applied on this class - the class has an empty content. |
| The UML datatypes in the transformation source set are transformed into the classes with the «XSDsimpleType» stereotype (unless after the type map this class appears to be derived from a class with the «XSDcomplexType» stereotype). | The «XSDcomplexType» stereotype is used. |
| Each attribute of the class is not of the simple XML type (that is, one of the basic types, or type, stereotyped with the «XSDsimpleType») or has a multiplicity more than 1. | The «XSDelement» stereotype is applied. |

- For each composition association, two linking classes are stereotyped as XML schema types. The stereotype on the association end is applied the same as the rules for attributes.
- Enumerations in the UML model are transformed into the enumerations in the XML Schema model. Classes with the «XSDsimpleType» stereotype are derived by restriction from the XML string type, where all the elements of the original enumeration are converted into the attributes with an «XSDenumeration» stereotype.
- For each package in the transformation set, the «XSDnamespace» stereotype is applied.
- In each package, one additional class for the XML schema is created. The name of the schema class is constructed by taking the name of the package and then appending the .xsd to it (e.g. if the package in the source model set is named "user", name the schema class "user.xsd" in the destination package).
- The targetNamespace value is added to the schema class with the name of its parent (e.g. if the schema is placed in the "http://magicdraw.com/User" package, the targetNamespace=" http://magicdraw.com/User" is set on the schema class).
- Schema class and the namespaces http://www.w3c.org/2001/XMLSchema [XML Schema profile] and its target namespace are linked using the xmlns relationships. The names of these links are: the same as the target namespace for the link to the target namespace; and "xs" for the XML Schema namespace.
- Class diagrams are transformed into XML Schema diagrams.
- The model elements that have no meaning in the XML schemas are discarded. This includes (without limitation) behavioral features of classes, interfaces, actors, use cases, states, activities, objects, messages, stereotypes and tag definitions.
- There are additional properties to choose for UML to XML Schema transformation in the Model Transformation Wizard. For more information about the wizard, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Transformation Wizard' space=''] in MagicDraw User Guide.

###### [IMAGE alt='' src='']Model transformation wizard for UML to XML schema transformation with Specify transformation details.

| Option name | Type | Description |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Default Compositor | Combo box | Possible choices: XSDall, XSDchoice, XSDsequenceDetermines element grouping in complex types of XML Schema.Default: XSDall |
| Default Attribute Kind | Combo box | Determines which attribute kind (XSDelement or XSDattribute UML) the attribute will be mapped to.Default: XSDelement |

919614709

**Related page**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Model Transformation Wizard' space='']

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243966514 space=MT version=4 -->
## PAGE 06511: Understanding cell content

- page_id: `243966514`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243966514/Understanding+cell+content
- version_number: 4
- version_date: `2025-08-25T12:20:23.180+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Diagrams > Analysis diagrams > Dependency Matrix > Using Dependency Matrix
- labels: ['msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

This page explains the content of the cell in the Dependency matrix. The following sections describe the Dependency matrix areas and cell content:

43

##### Rows, columns, and cells in the Dependency matrix

The Dependency matrix contains the following areas:

- Rows display elements specified in the Row Element Type box and Row Scope box of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency Matrix Criteria area' space=''] .
- Columns display elements specified in the Column Element Type box and Column Scope box of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency Matrix Criteria area' space=''] .
- *Cells* display dependencies between elements specified in the rows and columns. The content of the cells depends on selected criteria in the **Dependency Criteria** box and the **Direction** box of the [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency Matrix Criteria area' space='']. Tip[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Selecting criteria' space='']]]> [IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The main areas of the Dependency matrix. The illustration displays concepts from the SysML v1 Plugin.

##### Representation of the dependencies in a cell

The default representation of the dependencies in the cells includes the following:

- Arrowed diagonal . The direction of the arrow is from the client element to the supplier element.

#### NOTE: Arrowed diagonal representation

Arrowed diagonal representation

The representation of the arrowed diagonal is different when the same elements are connected through:

- One dependency , e.g., [ATTACHMENT filename='one_way_arrow.png'] .
- Two dependencies, e.g., [ATTACHMENT filename='two_arrows.png'] and [ATTACHMENT filename='two_arrows_different_direction.png'] .
- More than two dependencies, e.g., [ATTACHMENT filename='one_way_arrow_with_number.png'] , [ATTACHMENT filename='two_way_arrow_with_number.png'] .

- Diagonal . Elements depend on each other.

#### TIP: Tip

Tip

You can specify the colors and line styles used to represent the relationships in your dependency matrices. [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Specifying relationship line styles' space='']]]>

##### Numbers in the Dependency matrix

There are three types of numbers in the matrix (see the following figure):

1. The number of dependencies in the row.
2. The number of dependencies in the column.
3. The number of criteria in the cell.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

##### The details of a cell

Move your mouse over a cell to view details of the dependency (relationship) represented in the cell. A tooltip displays the information about the relationships.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

###### The ToolTip displays information about existing relations between Over Pressure Valve and Anto Black-flow Valve Blocks. The illustration displays concepts from SysML v1 Plugin.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Creating Dependency Matrix' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Using Dependency Matrix' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Dependency Matrix environment' space='']

**Sample model**

The model used in the figures of this page is the**dependencyMatrixSample**model. Download[ATTACHMENT filename='dependencyMatrixSample.mdzip'].

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243968554 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06512: Understanding change concept

- page_id: `243968554`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243968554/Understanding+change+concept
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:51:02.256+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Tools > Model Merge
- labels: ['change-concept', 'change-type', 'addition-change', 'deletion-change', 'modification-change', 'movement-change', 'order-change', 'change-states', 'dependent-changes', 'conflicting-changes', 'equivalent-changes', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Change is a difference, found between the ancestor and a contributor. Every change can be accepted or rejected, as well as have dependent changes. Changes can conflict with each other or be equivalent. Merging begins with building a composite change tree, which consists of a model, diagramming, and non-model changes.

Read the following section to get familiar with different change types.

##### **#additionchange**Addition change

If an element has been added to a contributor, an addition change occurs.

##### **#deletionchange**Deletion change

If an element has been removed from a contributor, a deletion change occurs.

##### **#modificationchange**Modification change

If an element property in a contributor has been modified, a modification change occurs.

If the **Is****Abstract** property value of a class in the ancestor had the default value *false* and the same property value in a contributor has been changed to *true*, a modification change occurs.

There are three types of modification changes:

- Addition modification change that occurs when a value is added to a property.
- Deletion modification change that occurs when a value is removed from a property.
- Replacement modification change that occurs when one value is replaced with another. This type of modification change occurs only for properties that have multiplicity less or equal to 1.

##### **#movementchange**Movement change

If an element owner has been changed in a contributor, a movement change occurs.

Let's say package A contains some class in the ancestor and package B contains the same class in a contributor. This means that the class has been moved from package A to package B in the contributor. This case is recognized as a movement change.

Another case of the movement change is when an attribute or an operation that has been owned by class A in the ancestor, becomes the attribute or an operation of class B in a contributor.

##### **#orderchange**Order change

If the order of elements has been changed in a contributor, an order change occurs. Order changes can occur on elements such as attributes, operations, and other ordered elements. Even if a single element in a collection has changed its place, the order change is applied to the entire collection. 
 
Since an element can have several ordered collections, several order changes can occur on a single element.

Let's say class A has attributed a, b, and c in the ancestor. The attribute c has been moved up and placed above attribute a in a contributor. This means that the order of attribute collection in class A has changed in the contributor. This is a case of the order change.

Order changes can be skipped while merging. For this, you need to specify the names of the properties whose order changes should not be detected in the**Do Not Detect Order Changes for**environment option. For more information, see[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Preparing for merge' space=''].

##### **#changestates**Change states

Every change, whether it is addition, modification, deletion, movement, or order change, can be either accepted or rejected. It is a change state.

All accepted changes will be incorporated into the target. Alternatively, they can be rejected and excluded from the target.

##### **#dependentchanges**Dependent changes

In some cases, other changes have to be accepted or rejected before accepting or rejecting a selected change. In other words, a selected change sometimes depends on other changes and is called a dependent change.

For a better understanding of the concept of dependent changes, study the following examples.

Let's say a class attribute type has been changed to a type that had been created by another change. In consequence, the attribute type change depends on the change that has created the type. This means that type creation change must be accepted before accepting type modification change.

Let's suppose there is an attribute type change in a contributor. An old type has been deleted and a new type has been added to the contributor. In this case, three changes occur:

- deletion change (for the old type)
- addition change (for the new type)
- modification change (for the property type)

These are also ownership changes, but they are accepted together with deletion and addition changes. 
The modification change depends on the addition change, and the deletion change depends on the modification change.Thus accepting the deletion change means also accepting the addition change, the modification change and the deletion change itself.

##### Conflicting changes

Conflict occurs when two changes are incompatible, i.e., the changes cannot be accepted together. Conflicts can only occur when using 3-way merge.

The following situations will result in a conflict:

- Each contributor has changed a class name or any other element property value.
- One contributor has added an operation to a class and the other contributor deleted the class.
- One contributor has moved a class into one package while the other contributor moved it to another package.

##### **#equivalentchanges**Equivalent changes

Equivalent change is a pair of identical changes that are detected in both source and target when performing a 3-way merge.

Let's say package A contains some class in the ancestor and both contributors contain the same class in package B. This means that the class has been moved from package A to package B in both source and target. This is a case of the equivalent change.

Another case of equivalent changes is when the same element A becomes renamed to B in both contributors.

**Related pages**

- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding merge types' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Preparing for merge' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Starting Model Merge' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Analyzing and managing merge results' space='']
- [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Finishing and canceling merge' space='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<ac:layout><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p>Change is a difference, found between the ancestor and a contributor. Every change can be accepted or rejected, as well as have dependent changes. Changes can conflict with each other or be equivalent. <span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Merging begins with building a composite change tree, which consists of a model, diagramming, and non-model changes.</span></p><p>Read the following section to get familiar with different change types.<span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> </span></p><h3><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="6547ebe1-3a4d-4b5d-a77b-48fe761f8e05"><ac:parameter ac:name="">#additionchange</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></span><span style="letter-spacing: -0.006em;">Addition change</span></h3><p>If an element has been added to a contributor, an addition change occurs.<span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> <br /></span></p><h3><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5eb13209-1e68-445b-9dd5-11f1bcda8973"><ac:parameter ac:name="">#deletionchange</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></span><span style="letter-spacing: -0.006em;">Deletion change</span></h3><p>If an element has been removed from a contributor, a deletion change occurs.  <span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><br /></span></p><h3><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="0ac23d21-cc7f-4937-88b3-42e018befea7"><ac:parameter ac:name="">#modificationchange</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></span><span style="letter-spacing: -0.006em;">Modification change</span></h3><p>If an element property in a contributor has been modified, a modification change occurs.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fe88152e-95da-4b3d-ae50-91886444d127"><ac:rich-text-body><p>If the <strong>Is</strong><strong>Abstract</strong> property value of a class in the ancestor had the default value <em>false</em> and the same property value in a contributor has been changed to <em>true</em>, a modification change occurs.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p>There are three types of modification changes:</p><ul><li><strong>Addition modification change</strong> that occurs when a value is added to a property.</li><li><strong>Deletion modification change</strong> that occurs when a value is removed from a property.</li><li><strong>Replacement modification change</strong> that occurs when one value is replaced with another. This type of modification change occurs only for properties that have multiplicity less or equal to 1.<span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> </span></li></ul><h3><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="07b2e240-b7fc-4685-baf7-25e88052aa5b"><ac:parameter ac:name="">#movementchange</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></span><span style="letter-spacing: -0.006em;">Movement change</span></h3><p>If an element owner has been changed in a contributor, a movement change occurs.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="89c94be7-2628-430f-a0ee-5caf9c4bb81f"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Let's say package A contains some class in the ancestor and package B contains the same class in a contributor. This means that the class has been moved from package A to package B in the contributor. This case is recognized as a movement change.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="f15971ff-0eb5-41f1-ab0b-140458a12568"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Another case of the movement change is when an attribute or an operation that has been owned by class A in the ancestor, becomes the attribute or an operation of class B in a contributor.<span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> </span></p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="09c18b65-c5d0-4e7d-8e62-a769a7a93f79"><ac:parameter ac:name="">#orderchange</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong></span><span style="letter-spacing: -0.006em;">Order change</span></h3><p>If the order of elements has been changed in a contributor, an order change occurs. Order changes can occur on elements such as attributes, operations, and other ordered elements. Even if a single element in a collection has changed its place, the order change is applied to the entire collection.<br /><br />Since an element can have several ordered collections, several order changes can occur on a single element.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="fb4a789a-a329-4927-aed5-dd1866d04476"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Let's say class A has attributed a, b, and c in the ancestor. The attribute c has been moved up and placed above attribute a in a contributor. This means that the order of attribute collection in class A has changed in the contributor. This is a case of the order change.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><p class="auto-cursor-target"><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Order changes can be skipped while merging. For this, you need to specify the names of the properties whose order changes should not be detected in the </span><strong style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">Do Not Detect Order Changes for</strong><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;"> environment option. For more information, see </span><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Preparing for merge" /></ac:link><span style="letter-spacing: 0.0px;">.</span></p><h3><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5cd9ebef-efce-404e-89be-4c75737fc847"><ac:parameter ac:name="">#changestates</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong>Change states</h3><p>Every change, whether it is addition, modification, deletion, movement, or order change, can be either accepted or rejected. It is a change state.</p><p>All accepted changes will be incorporated into the target. Alternatively, they can be rejected and excluded from the target.</p><h3><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="aa850e29-3bd2-4449-8a71-f33e8fafb206"><ac:parameter ac:name="">#dependentchanges</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong>Dependent changes</h3><p>In some cases, other changes have to be accepted or rejected before accepting or rejecting a selected change. In other words, a selected change sometimes depends on other changes and is called a dependent change.</p><p>For a better understanding of the concept of dependent changes, study the following examples.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="2e231999-6ec7-43d1-aa0a-7bbbb69b45ae"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Let's say a class attribute type has been changed to a type that had been created by another change. In consequence, the attribute type change depends on the change that has created the type. This means that type creation change must be accepted before accepting type modification change.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="022cfc69-c633-42b1-be98-179ea40707d4"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Let's suppose there is an attribute type change in a contributor. An old type has been deleted and a new type has been added to the contributor. In this case, three changes occur:</p><ul><li>deletion change (for the old type)</li><li>addition change (for the new type)</li><li>modification change (for the property type)</li></ul><p>These are also ownership changes, but they are accepted together with deletion and addition changes.<br />The modification change depends on the addition change, and the deletion change depends on the modification change.Thus accepting the deletion change means also accepting the addition change, the modification change and the deletion change itself.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3>Conflicting changes</h3><p>Conflict occurs when two changes are incompatible, i.e., the changes cannot be accepted together. Conflicts can only occur when using 3-way merge.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="de1e0e95-13ee-45c3-8c87-f1c412e9bfaf"><ac:rich-text-body><p>The following situations will result in a conflict:</p><ul><li>Each contributor has changed a class name or any other element property value.</li><li>One contributor has added an operation to a class and the other contributor deleted the class.</li><li>One contributor has moved a class into one package while the other contributor moved it to another package.</li></ul></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><h3><strong><ac:structured-macro ac:name="anchor" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="22c0bb61-77bd-4811-a52d-71f398b03479"><ac:parameter ac:name="">#equivalentchanges</ac:parameter></ac:structured-macro></strong>Equivalent changes</h3><p>Equivalent change is a pair of identical changes that are detected in both source and target when performing a 3-way merge.</p><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="5d46f7e7-ee62-4949-9153-a75ff54171b5"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Let's say package A contains some class in the ancestor and both contributors contain the same class in package B. This means that the class has been moved from package A to package B in both source and target. This is a case of the equivalent change.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro><ac:structured-macro ac:name="tip" ac:schema-version="1" ac:macro-id="e60837bb-231f-40fe-afd6-272ff1def8c4"><ac:rich-text-body><p>Another case of equivalent changes is when the same element A becomes renamed to B in both contributors.</p></ac:rich-text-body></ac:structured-macro></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p><br /></p></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section><ac:layout-section ac:type="single"><ac:layout-cell><p class="auto-cursor-target"><strong>Related pages</strong></p><ul class="childpages-macro conf-macro output-block"><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Understanding merge types" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Preparing for merge" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Starting Model Merge" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Analyzing and managing merge results" /></ac:link></li><li><ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Finishing and canceling merge" /></ac:link></li></ul></ac:layout-cell></ac:layout-section></ac:layout>
````

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243969416 space=MT version=1 -->
## PAGE 06513: Understanding change types

- page_id: `243969416`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243969416/Understanding+change+types
- version_number: 1
- version_date: `2025-07-31T10:51:28.262+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Model analysis > Comparing projects
- labels: ['change-concept', 'addition-change', 'deletion-change', 'modification-change', 'movement-change', 'order-change', 'dependent-changes', 'change-types', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Change is a difference, found between the base and compared project versions.

Read the following definitions to get familiar with different change types.

##### Addition change

If an element has been added to a compared project version, an addition change occurs.

##### Deletion change

If an element has been removed from a compared project version, a deletion change occurs.

##### Modification change

If an element property in a compared project version has been modified, a modification change occurs.

If the **Is****Abstract** property value of a class in the base had the default value *false* and the same property value in a compared project version has been changed to *true*, a modification change occurs.

There are three types of modification changes:

- **Addition modification change** that occurs when a value is added to a property.
- **Deletion modification change** that occurs when a value is removed from a property.
- **Replacement modification change** that occurs when one value is replaced with another. This type of modification change occurs only for properties that have multiplicity less or equal to 1.

##### Movement change

If an element owner has been changed in a compared project version, a movement change occurs.

Let's say package A contains some class in the base and package B contains the same class in a compared project version. This means that the class has been moved from package A to package B in the compared project version. This case is recognized as a movement change.

Another case of the movement change is when an attribute or an operation that has been owned by class A in the base, becomes the attribute or an operation of class B in a compared project version.

##### Order change

If the order of elements has been changed in a compared project version, an order change occurs. Order changes can occur on elements such as attributes, operations, and other ordered elements. Even if a single element in a collection has changed its place, the order change is applied to the entire collection. 
Since an element can have several ordered collections, several order changes can occur on a single element.

Let's say class A has attributes a, b, and c in the base. The attribute c has been moved up and placed above attribute a in a compared project version. This means that the order of attribute collection in class A has changed in the compared project version. This is a case of the order change.

Order changes can be skipped while comparing projects. For this, you need to specify the names of properties whose order changes should not be detected in the **Do Not Detect Order Changes for** environment option. For more information, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Preparing for project comparison' space=''].

##### Dependent change

In some cases, changes depend on other changes and are called dependent changes.For better understanding of the concept of dependent changes, study the following examples.

Let's say a class attribute type has been changed to a type that had been created by another change. In consequence, the attribute type change depends on the change that has created the type.

Let's suppose there is an attribute type change in a compared project version. An old type has been deleted and a new type has been added to the compared project version. In this case, three changes occur:

- deletion change (for the old type)
- addition change (for the new type)
- modification change (for the property type)

The modification change depends on the addition change, and the deletion change depends on the modification change.

<!--NOMAGIC_PAGE id=243969428 space=MT version=2 -->
## PAGE 06514: Understanding content of the Differences tree

- page_id: `243969428`
- space_key: `MT`
- source_url: https://docs.nomagic.com/spaces/MT/pages/243969428/Understanding+content+of+the+Differences+tree
- version_number: 2
- version_date: `2025-08-07T17:23:59.093+02:00`
- ancestors: Modeling Tools Documentation > General modeling features > Model analysis > Comparing projects > Analyzing comparison results
- labels: ['element-decorations', 'recognizing-changes', 'comparison-result', 'msosa', 'mcse', 'msa']

### NORMALIZED CONTENT

Elements with all change types are displayed in the **Differences**tree that is located at the top left of the **Difference Viewer** window.

**Used project representation**

This node indicates that the compared project contains modified used projects or used projects with modified usages.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Recognizing addition changes**

For the addition change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change types' space=''].

Added elements are highlighted in green background. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Recognizing deletion changes**

For the deletion change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change types' space=''].

Deleted elements are highlighted in red background. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Recognizing modification changes**

For the modification change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change types' space=''].

Elements whose properties have been changed are highlighted in blue. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
To see what properties have been modified, click the element and see changes in the **Specification** panel.

For more information about using the Specification panel, see [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Inspecting differences in the Change details panel' space=''].

**Recognizing movement changes**

For the movement change description, refer to[CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change types' space=''].

Elements with movement changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes, since movement changes are some kind of modification changes. Icon of an element with movement changes is represented with arrow symbol on the left.

The following figure illustrates a movement change, when element X has been moved from package A to package B.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

You can navigate from the element's original location to the new one (and vice versa) using the commands on the element shortcut menu.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
**Recognizing order changes**

For the order change description, refer to [CONFLUENCE_PAGE title='Understanding change types' space=''].

Elements with order changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes, since order changes are some kind of modification changes. 
 
[IMAGE alt='' src='']

**Recognizing elements with changed owned elements** 
Elements whose owned elements (in any nesting level) have changes are highlighted using red diagonals.

[IMAGE alt='' src=''] 
 
 Modified elements with changed owned elements are highlighted in blue with red diagonals.

[IMAGE alt='' src='']

### EXACT CONFLUENCE STORAGE SOURCE

````html
<p>Elements with all change types are displayed in the <strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="8447146e-3155-48f8-86fb-854627acae30">Differences</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="8447146e-3155-48f8-86fb-854627acae30"> tree</ac:inline-comment-marker> that is located at the top left of the <strong>Difference Viewer</strong> window.</p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"> <strong>Used project representation</strong></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="05eb7d70-6c53-4197-863f-b315fa20a081">This node indicates that the compared project contains modified used projects or used projects with <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="f9feffa0-aeb7-429b-b4d2-6c1fc64dd405">modified usages</ac:inline-comment-marker>.</ac:inline-comment-marker></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(255,0,255);"><ac:image ac:title="Used project representation" ac:alt="Used project representation" ac:height="63" ac:width="385"><ri:attachment ri:filename="non_model_concepts_.png" /></ac:image><br /></span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Recognizing addition changes</strong></span></p><p>For the addition change description, refer to <ac:link ac:anchor="additionchange"><ri:page ri:content-title="Understanding change types" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Addition change]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="b6cc6233-88ad-4418-910e-88b6457cf57b">.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p>Added <ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="535909c8-6c39-49cc-9bb3-9a4a860bf8e1">elements</ac:inline-comment-marker> are highlighted in green background.<br /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:title="Example of addition change" ac:alt="Example of addition change" ac:height="68" ac:width="252"><ri:attachment ri:filename="added_elements.png"><ri:page ri:content-title="_IMG" /></ri:attachment></ac:image></p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Recognizing deletion changes</strong></span></p><p>For the deletion change description, refer to <ac:link ac:anchor="deletionchange"><ri:page ri:content-title="Understanding change types" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Deletion change]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="d8978862-44e9-4193-a0bf-13715cd97a04">.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p>Deleted elements are highlighted in red background. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Example of deletion change" ac:alt="Example of deletion change"><ri:attachment ri:filename="deletion_change.png" /></ac:image><br /><span style="color: rgb(129,137,156);"> </span></p><p><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Recognizing modification changes</strong></span></p><p>For the modification change description, refer to <ac:link ac:anchor="modificationchange"><ri:page ri:content-title="Understanding change types" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Modification change]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="40e1100a-0aef-44a5-905c-53668fc7bac1">.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p>Elements whose properties have been changed are highlighted in blue. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br /><ac:image ac:title="Element with modified properties" ac:alt="Element with modified properties"><ri:attachment ri:filename="element_modified_properties.png" /></ac:image><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br />To see what properties have been modified, click the element and see changes in the <strong>Specification</strong> panel.</p><p>For more information about using the Specification panel, see <ac:link><ri:page ri:content-title="Inspecting differences in the Change details panel" /></ac:link>.</p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a9d8377d-0613-483d-ae5f-ca06c0bc8272">Recognizing movement changes</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong></span></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a9d8377d-0613-483d-ae5f-ca06c0bc8272">For the movement change description, refer to </ac:inline-comment-marker><ac:link ac:anchor="movementchange"><ri:page ri:content-title="Understanding change types" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Movement change]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="1344219e-85f4-4ce0-aa0f-5cb6a51585d4"><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a9d8377d-0613-483d-ae5f-ca06c0bc8272">.</ac:inline-comment-marker></ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a9d8377d-0613-483d-ae5f-ca06c0bc8272">Elements with movement changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes, since movement changes are some kind of modification changes. Icon of an element with movement changes is represented with arrow symbol on the left.</ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="a9d8377d-0613-483d-ae5f-ca06c0bc8272">The following figure illustrates a movement change, when element X has been moved from package A to package B.<br /></ac:inline-comment-marker></p><p><ac:image ac:title="Example of a movement change" ac:alt="Example of a movement change"><ri:attachment ri:filename="movement_change.png" /></ac:image></p><p><span>You can navigate from the element's original location to the new one (and vice versa) using the commands on the element shortcut menu.</span></p><p><ac:image ac:title="Navigating to new element location" ac:alt="Navigating to new element location"><ri:attachment ri:filename="nav_new_element_location.png" /></ac:image></p><p><ac:image ac:title="Navigating to original element location" ac:alt="Navigating to original element location"><ri:attachment ri:filename="nav_original_element_location.png" /></ac:image><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Recognizing order changes</strong></span></p><p>For the order change description, refer to <ac:link ac:anchor="orderchange"><ri:page ri:content-title="Understanding change types" /><ac:plain-text-link-body><![CDATA[Order change]]></ac:plain-text-link-body></ac:link>.</p><p>Elements with order changes are highlighted in the same blue background that is used to highlight modification changes, since order changes are some kind of modification changes. <br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:image ac:title="Example of accepted order change" ac:alt="Example of accepted order change"><ri:attachment ri:filename="example_order_change.png" /></ac:image></p><p><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><span style="color: rgb(51,51,51);"><strong>Re<ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="782f0a10-bbb6-4f1d-869c-5f86bb8cf0ee">cognizing elements with changed owned elements</ac:inline-comment-marker></strong></span><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><ac:inline-comment-marker ac:ref="782f0a10-bbb6-4f1d-869c-5f86bb8cf0ee"> Elements whose owned elements (in any nesting level) have changes are highlig</ac:inline-comment-marker>hted using red diagonals.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Element with owned elements added" ac:alt="Element with owned elements added"><ri:attachment ri:filename="owned elements added.png" /></ac:image><br class="atl-forced-newline" /><br class="atl-forced-newline" /> Modified elements with changed owned elements are highlighted in blue with red diagonals.</p><p><ac:image ac:title="Element with owned elements added and modification change" ac:alt="Element with owned elements added and modification change"><ri:attachment ri:filename="owned elements added&amp;modification change.png" /></ac:image></p><p> </p>
````
